section.title: The New Jerusalem
section.sectionRationale: Where 21:1-8 declared the new creation in overview from the throne, one of the seven bowl angels now carries John away for a guided, measured tour of the bride city itself. The unit is bounded by the angel's invitation, 'Come, I will show you the bride,' and closes with the vision's own resolution, no more curse, unbroken worship, and an eternal reign, before the epilogue's final words begin in 22:6. It is the single most sustained description of the eternal state in Scripture, and it forms one coherent scene of showing and measuring.
section.thesis: The bride, the holy city, descends in full splendor, gathering up temple, garden, and city imagery at once: no temple, because God and the Lamb are its temple; no curse, because the tree of life stands open again; and no night, because the Lord God is its everlasting light.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the bride, the holy city, shown descending in glory with its named gates and foundations; the angel measuring the city's dimensions and cataloguing its gold, gems, and pearls; the declaration that there is no temple and no night, because God and the Lamb are the city's temple and lamp; the river of the water of life and the tree of life flowing from the throne; and the closing promise of no more curse, unbroken worship, and an everlasting reign.

verses.text: Then one of the seven angels with the seven bowls full of the seven final plagues came and said to me, “Come, I will show you the {{nymphe|bride}}, the wife of the Lamb.”
And he carried me away in the Spirit to a mountain great and high, and showed me the holy city of Jerusalem coming down out of heaven from God,
shining with the glory of God. Its radiance was like a most precious jewel, like a jasper, as clear as crystal.
The city had a great and high wall with twelve gates inscribed with the names of the twelve tribes of Israel, and twelve angels at the gates.
There were three gates on the east, three on the north, three on the south, and three on the west.
The wall of the city had twelve foundations bearing the names of the twelve apostles of the Lamb.

units.label: Measuring the city of gold and jewels
units.note: a golden rod, a perfect cube

verses.text: The angel who spoke with me had a golden measuring rod to measure the city and its gates and walls.
The city lies foursquare, with its width the same as its length. And he measured the city with the rod, and all its dimensions were equal: 12,000 stadia in length and width and height.
And he measured its wall to be 144 cubits, by the human measure the angel was using.
The wall was made of jasper, and the city itself of pure gold, as pure as glass.
The foundations of the city walls were adorned with every kind of precious stone: the first foundation was jasper, the second sapphire, the third chalcedony, the fourth emerald,
the fifth sardonyx, the sixth carnelian, the seventh chrysolite, the eighth beryl, the ninth topaz, the tenth chrysoprase, the eleventh jacinth, and the twelfth amethyst.
And the twelve gates were twelve pearls, with each gate consisting of a single pearl. The main street of the city was pure gold, as clear as glass.

units.label: No temple, for God and the Lamb are its temple

verses.text: But I saw no {{naos|temple}} in the city, because the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are its {{naos|temple}}.
And the city has no need of sun or moon to shine on it, because the glory of God illuminates the city, and the Lamb is its lamp.
By its light the nations will walk, and into it the kings of the earth will bring their glory.
Its gates will never be shut at the end of the day, because there will be no night there.
And into the city will be brought the glory and honor of the nations.
But nothing unclean will ever enter it, nor anyone who practices an abomination or a lie, but only those whose names are written in the Lamb’s Book of Life.

units.label: The river and the tree of life

verses.text: Then the angel showed me a river of the water of life, as clear as crystal, flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb

down the middle of the main street of the city. On either side of the river stood a {{xylon_zoes|tree of life}}, bearing twelve kinds of fruit and yielding a fresh crop for each month. And the leaves of the tree are for the healing of the nations.

units.label: No more curse, they will reign forever

verses.text: No longer will there be any {{katathema|curse}}. The throne of God and of the Lamb will be within the city, and His servants will {{latreuo|worship}} Him.
They will see His face, and His name will be on their foreheads.
There will be no more night in the city, and they will have no need for the light of a lamp or of the sun. For the Lord God will shine on them, and they will reign forever and ever.

nymphe.gloss: Bride, or young wife. The church is shown as the bride, the wife of the Lamb, the consummation of the marriage supper announced in Revelation 19:7-9. It gathers up the covenant marriage imagery used of Israel and the church throughout Scripture into one final, descending city.

naos.gloss: The inner sanctuary or temple proper, the innermost dwelling place of God, distinct from the wider temple courts. Its total absence here is itself the fulfillment: God and the Lamb are the naos, so there is no longer any localized, separate sanctuary, only unmediated presence filling the whole city.

xylon_zoes.gloss: Tree of life, xylon meaning wood or tree, zoes the genitive of life. It recalls the tree of Genesis 2:9, once guarded from fallen humanity by a flaming sword, now standing open on both sides of the river, its leaves given for healing rather than judgment.

katathema.gloss: An accursed or banned thing, a word related to anathema. It is distinct from katara, the more common New Testament word for curse used, for example, in Galatians 3:13. Its complete absence here declares the reversal of the curse placed on the ground and on humanity in Genesis 3:17-19.

latreuo.gloss: To render priestly service or worship, the word used throughout the Greek Old Testament for Israel's tabernacle and temple service. Here it names the unbroken, priestly worship of God's servants who see His face directly, distinct from proskyneo, to bow down, though both belong to the redeemed.

basic.context: An angel carries John to a high mountain to show him the bride, the holy city, descending from heaven in dazzling glory. He measures its walls, gates, and foundations, all named for the twelve tribes and the twelve apostles, built of gold, jewels, and pearl.
basic.hermeneutics: Readers debate whether the city's exact measurements and jewels describe a literal building or are rich symbols, especially since the city is a perfect cube, the same shape as the Holy of Holies in Solomon's temple. Either way, the point is clear: full, ordered, priestly access to God for His whole people forever.
basic.originalLanguages: The bride, nymphe, is the church, the wife of the Lamb. There is no naos, temple, because God and the Lamb are the temple. The xylon zoes, tree of life, once barred, now grows freely, and there is no more katathema, curse, only unbroken latreuo, worship.
basic.typology: The tree of life barred in Eden is freely accessible again; the whole city is shaped like the Most Holy Place, once entered only once a year by one priest; and the bride city fulfills the marriage covenant God made with His people from Hosea to Ephesians.
basic.christ: The Lamb shares the very throne of God, is the city's lamp and its temple, and is Bridegroom to the bride shown here; His name is written on His people's foreheads, and it is by His blood, named just before this vision, that anyone's name stands in the Book of Life.
basic.god: God and the Lamb together are the city's light, its temple, and its throne; His unmediated presence, once approached only through priest, veil, and blood, is now the whole city's atmosphere, and His people will see His face and reign with Him forever.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Kings 6:20 describes the cube-shaped Holy of Holies. Exodus 28:17-21 lists the twelve jewels of the priestly breastpiece. Isaiah 60 promises nations walking by Zion's light and kings bringing their glory. Genesis 2-3 is the tree of life and the curse this vision reverses.
basic.oneStory: Eden had one tree of life, barred by a flaming sword and cherubim after the fall. Here the tree grows freely on both sides of a river flowing straight from God's throne, and there is no more curse at all, the ancient exile fully and finally undone.

context.literary: The most extended, carefully measured description of the eternal state in Scripture, framed by an angel's invitation to see the bride, in 21:9, and the vision's own resolution of no curse and endless reign, in 22:3-5. It blends bridal, urban, and temple imagery into a single scene, moving from a wide view of the descending city to a detailed inventory of its walls and gates, and finally into its inner life of light, river, and throne.
context.historical: For churches who knew Jerusalem's earthly temple destroyed or under threat, and who lived among the great, glittering cities of the Roman world, this vision answers both losses: a city more glorious than Rome, and a dwelling of God greater than any temple that could be built or torn down.
context.canonical: Draws together the tabernacle and temple instructions of Exodus and Ezekiel, the marriage covenant of Hosea and Ephesians, and the tree of life and the curse from Genesis 2-3, weaving the whole Bible's architecture, garden, and bridal imagery into one final scene.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show suffering, exiled believers the full splendor of their eternal home: a city where God's own presence is the temple, the light, and the endless day, and where access to Him, once guarded and mediated, is now full and unbroken for all His people.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes what will be, not a plan to be built or a code to be decoded; its abiding force is the assurance of unmediated fellowship with God and the call, echoed in 21:27, to be among those whose names are written in the Lamb's Book of Life rather than those excluded.

debates.question: Are the New Jerusalem's measurements, 12,000 stadia and 144 cubits, and its jewels literal architecture, or symbolic of something else?

views.label: A literal, physical city of these dimensions
views.case: John records precise numbers, taken with an actual measuring rod, as one would expect of a real, described place; reading the numbers plainly honors the text's own claim to have been measured and witnessed by an eyewitness.

views.label: Rich symbolism drawn from the Holy of Holies and the twelve tribes and apostles
views.case: The city is a perfect cube, 12,000 stadia in every direction, the very shape of the Most Holy Place in Solomon's temple, 1 Kings 6:20, and its measurements are saturated with the number twelve, tribes, apostles, gates, foundations, squared and multiplied; on this reading the whole city is one vast Holy of Holies, symbolizing that the entire dwelling of God's people is now what only the inmost sanctuary once was.

debates.bearing: Either reading arrives at the same truth: perfect, ordered, priestly access to God for the whole covenant community forever. Interpreters differ over whether to press the numbers as a literal blueprint or read them as a rich symbolic statement that God's presence has expanded from one small room to fill the entire dwelling of His people.

study.originalLanguages: The angel shows John the nymphe, the bride, the church presented as the Lamb's own wife. Walking through the city, John notes there is no naos, no inner sanctuary, because God Almighty and the Lamb are themselves the naos; the whole city has become what the Holy of Holies once was. Flowing from the throne is a river, and on either side stands the xylon zoes, the tree of life, its wood and fruit now open to all rather than guarded from any. No katathema, no accursed or banned thing, remains, the ancient curse of Genesis 3 undone, and God's servants latreuo, render Him unbroken priestly worship, seeing His face without veil, mediator, or distance.

typology.type: The tree of life barred in Eden, Genesis 3:22-24
typology.fulfillment: Freely accessible again on both sides of the river of life, Revelation 22:2, its leaves now for healing rather than being guarded by a flaming sword.

typology.type: The Holy of Holies, a perfect cube, 1 Kings 6:20
typology.fulfillment: The whole city takes the shape of the Most Holy Place, the entire dwelling of God's people made into the innermost sanctuary.

typology.type: Israel betrothed to the LORD, and the church as Christ's bride, Hosea 2:19-20, Ephesians 5:25-27
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the bride, the wife of the Lamb, the New Jerusalem descending in glory to meet her husband.

typology.type: The high priest's breastpiece of twelve jewels, Exodus 28:17-21
typology.fulfillment: Echoed in the twelve jeweled foundations of the city, the whole priestly people now adorned as the high priest once was alone.

study.christ: The Lamb is named again and again alongside God Himself: He shares the very throne from which the river of life flows, He is the city's lamp in place of sun and moon, and He together with God the Father is its temple. He is Bridegroom to the bride shown descending, and it is only those written in His Book of Life, secured by His own blood, who may enter what he called at the cross 'It is finished.' His servants will see His face, the very thing Moses was told no one could do and live, now granted forever.
study.god: God and the Lamb together are the city's unshielded light, its throne, and its temple; the presence once approached only through priest, veil, and sacrificial blood now fills the whole city as its very atmosphere. He receives the glory and honor the nations bring, He excludes all that is unclean, and He grants His people to see His face and reign with Him forever and ever.

crossReferences.note: The Most Holy Place was built as a perfect cube, the same shape given to the entire New Jerusalem.
The twelve jewels of the high priest's breastpiece, echoed in the city's twelve foundation stones.
Nations walk by Zion's light, her gates stand open, kings bring their wealth, and the LORD becomes her everlasting light.
The tree of life and the curse on the ground, both directly reversed in this vision.

study.oneStory: Eden held one tree of life, guarded after the fall by cherubim and a flaming sword, and the ground itself lay under a curse. Here the tree grows freely on both sides of a river flowing straight from the throne of God and the Lamb, there is no more curse at all, and God's servants see His face and reign with Him forever, the ancient exile from Genesis 3 fully and finally undone in the descent of the bride, the holy city.

sermon.title: The New Jerusalem
sermon.why: Preached in the series Revelation: The Final Word on exactly this passage, Revelation 21:1-22:5, Keller unfolds the descending bride city and its restoration of Eden as the church's certain future hope of death conquered and all things restored.

quiz.q: Why does John see no temple in the New Jerusalem, Revelation 21:22?

options.text: Because the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are themselves its temple.
options.note: This is stated directly in verse 22.

options.text: Because the city was too small to contain a temple building.
options.note: The reason given is theological, God and the Lamb are the temple, not a matter of size.

options.text: Because worship has ended entirely in the new creation.
options.note: Worship continues unbroken, Revelation 22:3, it simply needs no separate building.

options.text: Because the city belongs only to angels, not to redeemed people.
options.note: The city is the dwelling of God's redeemed people, His servants who worship Him there.

quiz.q: What does the city's cube shape, 12,000 stadia in every direction, most likely recall?

options.text: The Holy of Holies, the cube-shaped Most Holy Place of Solomon's temple, 1 Kings 6:20.
options.note: Many interpreters read the cube as identifying the whole city with the innermost sanctuary.

options.text: A literal city plan with no symbolic significance at all.
options.note: Even readers who take the numbers literally recognize the deliberate echo of the Holy of Holies' shape.

options.text: The size of ancient Rome, which the city is meant to outmatch.
options.note: The shape's significance lies in temple imagery, not a comparison to Rome's dimensions.

options.text: Nothing in particular; cube shapes have no meaning in Scripture.
options.note: The Most Holy Place was built as a cube, making the echo unmistakable.

quiz.q: What is significant about the twelve gates named for the tribes and the twelve foundations named for the apostles?

options.text: They join the Old and New Covenant people of God into one city, one people.
options.note: Israel's tribes and the Lamb's apostles together form the city's structure.

options.text: They show that only Israel, not the church, will dwell in the city.
options.note: Both tribes and apostles are named together, uniting rather than excluding either group.

options.text: They are decorative details with no connection to God's people.
options.note: The names directly identify God's covenant people across both testaments.

options.text: They indicate exactly twenty-four literal individuals will live there.
options.note: The names represent the whole believing community of both covenants, not a headcount.

quiz.q: What flows from the throne of God and of the Lamb in Revelation 22:1-2?

options.text: A river of the water of life, with the tree of life growing on both its banks.
options.note: This matches the vision exactly.

options.text: A river of fire consuming the unrighteous.
options.note: That is a different image found elsewhere in Revelation; here the river is the water of life, a picture of blessing.

options.text: A road leading out of the city toward the nations.
options.note: The text describes a river and a tree, not a road.

options.text: Nothing; the throne is described as dry and empty.
options.note: The throne is the very source of the river of life.

quiz.q: What does 'no longer will there be any curse,' Revelation 22:3, mean for God's people?

options.text: The curse placed on the ground and on humanity after the fall, Genesis 3:17-19, is fully reversed.
options.note: This directly answers the judgments of Genesis 3.

options.text: Only physical illness will end, while spiritual separation from God continues.
options.note: The removal of the curse includes unbroken worship and seeing God's face, not physical healing alone.

options.text: Some believers will still live under partial judgment.
options.note: The text states no curse will remain at all for God's people in the city.

options.text: The curse simply moves from the earth to the New Jerusalem.
options.note: The city is precisely where the curse is declared absent.

questions: The bride, the holy city, is shown as radiant with the very glory of God. What would it look like for you to let that future glory shape how you see your present, ordinary life?
There is no temple because God and the Lamb are the temple, no more distance between His people and Himself. Where do you still feel distant from God, and how does this vision speak to that?
The tree of life, once barred in Eden, now stands open on both sides of the river. What does it mean to you that access to life with God is now a gift freely given, not a barrier to overcome?
'No longer will there be any curse.' What burden, brokenness, or grief in your life right now most needs to hear that promise?
God's people will see His face and reign with Him forever. How does that future hope change the way you face this week's disappointments or delays?

translationNotes.note: 12,000 stadia and 144 cubits are both built from the number twelve, twelve squared multiplied by a thousand, and twelve times twelve, numbers that saturate the vision with the completeness of God's covenant people rather than functioning as an ordinary architect's plan.

Jasper here is likely a clear or translucent stone rather than the opaque stone known by that name today, since it is repeatedly compared to crystal and glass; ancient stone names did not always match modern mineral classifications.
The Lamb's Book of Life was introduced earlier in Revelation, 13:8 and 20:12, 15; its mention here ties this vision of the city's purity to the final judgment scene that precedes it.

'The leaves of the tree are for the healing of the nations' does not imply sickness remains in the new creation; it pictures the tree's abundance as continual life and flourishing for all peoples, not a remedy for ongoing disease.

meta.notes: BSB text supplied and pre-verified this session; no em or en dashes were present in the supplied text and none were introduced. Poetic line breaks were already flattened into flowing prose as supplied, and straight quotation marks were converted to curly typographic marks with no change to wording. The passage spans two chapters; the chapter field in the verses array correctly reads 21 for verses 9 through 27 and switches to 22 for verses 1 through 5, with the switch occurring precisely between Revelation 21:27 and 22:1 as required.
